does: This paper introduces Simulate and Eliminate (SANDE), a method to remove backdoors from generative large language models that have been poisoned during pre-training. The approach combines two key techniques: first, it simulates potential backdoor triggers by generating candidate trigger patterns, and second, it uses Overwrite Supervised Fine-tuning (OSFT) to explicitly teach the model to produce benign outputs when these triggers are encountered, thereby erasing the malicious backdoor mappings. The method addresses a critical gap where standard safety approaches like supervised fine-tuning and RLHF fail to eliminate backdoors already embedded in pre-trained models.
connects: This work extends research on both adversarial robustness in machine learning and LLM safety. It builds on earlier backdoor attack research that demonstrated vulnerabilities in neural networks to data poisoning, and responds directly to recent findings from Anthropic showing that conventional LLM alignment techniques cannot remove established backdoors. The paper connects to broader work on trigger detection and elimination in neural networks, while also engaging with the growing literature on LLM safety and trustworthiness that emerged as these models became more widely deployed. It addresses the specific problem of pre-training stage attacks that existing defense mechanisms overlook.
enables: This work enables more robust defenses against backdoor attacks in LLMs and points toward practical protocols for identifying and remediating poisoned models after deployment. It opens questions about detecting unknown triggers and scaling backdoor removal to very large models. The approach could inform the development of better verification and testing procedures for pre-trained models before fine-tuning, and might lead to more comprehensive safety training pipelines that simultaneously address multiple failure modes. It also enables further research into characterizing which backdoor attacks are most resilient and developing more sophisticated elimination strategies, potentially informing standards for responsible model release and third-party model auditing.
